does: This paper develops a comprehensive insurance framework for agentic AI systems, which differ fundamentally from traditional AI tools by autonomously planning, invoking tools, executing decisions, and modifying digital and physical environments. The authors characterize agentic AI along a continuum of autonomy and delegated authority, then systematically analyze major risk pathways including hallucinations, prompt-injection attacks, autonomous decision errors, model drift, dependency failures, and cyber-physical harms. The work evaluates how existing insurance products—cyber, professional liability, product liability, and directors and officers coverage—are inadequate for these novel exposures and develops implications for underwriting, pricing, reinsurance, and product design specific to agentic systems.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining AI system risks and failure modes, extending beyond the informational-output focus of earlier work to address systems with material environmental impact. It connects to literature on autonomous systems governance, AI safety concerns including alignment failures and robustness issues, and emerging regulatory frameworks for high-stakes AI deployment. The paper also responds to practitioner work on AI risk management and the inadequacy of traditional insurance categories for novel AI-driven exposures. It synthesizes insights from cyber insurance, product liability, and professional indemnity markets while addressing their fundamental misalignment with agentic AI's unique risk profile of independent event generation through external actions.
enables: This framework enables the development of new insurance products and underwriting standards tailored to agentic AI, establishing baselines for risk assessment, monitoring, and pricing in this emerging market. The work points toward future research on measurable autonomy metrics, dependencies between agentic systems, and systemic risks from coordinated autonomous agents. It supports the creation of specialized coverage tiers and risk mitigation requirements, potentially driving demand for AI safety auditing, real-time monitoring, and governance mechanisms. The analysis also enables policymakers and insurers to anticipate future harm scenarios as autonomy increases, establishing foundations for regulatory alignment and insurance market maturation as agentic AI becomes more prevalent in critical infrastructure and business operations.
